chunk_summary: This section defines enteral and parenteral nutrition, outlines a heuristic for route selection in sick children, and emphasizes timely planning for nutrition support when intake is inadequate for >48–72 hours.
learning_objectives: Understand the definitions and indications for enteral and parenteral nutrition.
Learn the practical heuristic for selecting a nutrition route in pediatric patients.
Recognize the importance of documenting a nutrition plan for children with negligible intake for >48–72 hours.
Identify factors influencing the choice of feeding method, including gut functionality and aspiration risk.
key_facts: Enteral nutrition (EN) is preferred when the gut is functional, as it supports mucosal integrity and reduces reliance on central venous access.
Parenteral nutrition (PN) is indicated when EN is not feasible due to gastrointestinal immaturity, malabsorption, or contraindications to gut feeding.
A practical route-selection heuristic includes assessing gut perfusion, aspiration risk, delivery method, and duration of support.
Children with negligible intake for >48–72 hours should have a documented nutrition route, starting dose, target dose, and monitoring schedule.
The decision-making process prioritizes safety and feasibility, with EN being the default unless contraindicated.
reference_quote: “Use the gut if it works” remains the default because enteral feeding supports mucosal integrity and reduces reliance on central venous access.
document_key_topics: Definitions of enteral and parenteral nutrition
Heuristic for route selection in pediatric patients
Timing and planning for nutrition support
